explanation:
Modern study techniques are grounded in cognitive science research from scholars like Ebbinghaus, Bjork, and Dunlosky. Techniques such as spaced repetition, active recall, and interleaving have strong empirical support.

## How It Calculates

This calculator estimates your study hours based on:
- **Number of pages**: The volume of material to cover
- **Difficulty level**: The complexity of the subject matter
- **Retention goal**: Your target level of information retention

The calculator applies proven formulas and alerts you if values fall outside reasonable ranges.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a guideline estimate. For critical decisions (academic, financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: How many hours should I study per page?
faq.a: It depends on subject difficulty. Complex material (science, math) typically requires 1–2 hours per page, while easier subjects (history, literature) may need 30–45 minutes per page.

faq.q: What's the difference between spaced repetition and cramming?
faq.a: Spaced repetition spreads study sessions over time for stronger retention. Cramming fits all study into one session, resulting in weaker long-term memory. Research shows spaced repetition is significantly more effective.

faq.q: How does difficulty level affect study time?
faq.a: Higher difficulty increases study time requirements. This calculator adjusts estimates based on subject complexity—harder material requires more hours to achieve the same retention level.
This calculator provides a guideline estimate based on research-backed study science. Actual study time varies by individual learning style, prior knowledge, and study method quality.

faq.q: Can I use this for any subject?
faq.a: Yes. While study time varies by subject type (STEM usually requires more time), this calculator works across all disciplines. Adjust the difficulty level to match your subject.

faq.q: What study techniques give the best results?
faq.a: Research-backed techniques include: spaced repetition (reviewing material at increasing intervals), active recall (testing yourself), and interleaving (mixing different topics). Combine these for best retention.
